---
title: "006 — EV tipping points: threshold regression per state"
date: 2026-06-12
---

## Question

Is there a share level beyond which EV adoption **feeds on itself** —
chargers become viable, EVs visible, resale trusted — so growth accelerates
without fresh policy? Or does growth **bend down** (saturation) as the
early-adopter pool drains?

## Background — thresholds as the signature of feedback

In complex adaptive systems, positive feedback shows up as *convexity*:
the change in adoption (Δs) rises more than linearly with the level (s).
We test it with a hinge regression per state:

$$\Delta s_t = a + b\,s_{t-1} + c\,\max(0,\, s_{t-1}-\tau) + \varepsilon$$

Grid-search τ; a **positive** hinge coefficient `c` means growth accelerates
past τ (tipping); a **negative** one means deceleration past τ (saturation).
The SSE gain over a straight line tells us whether the threshold is real
structure. Shares are 3-month smoothed to avoid mean-reversion artefacts.

```{python}
import duckdb
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import complexity_lab.viz  # lab plotly template
from complexity_lab.config import settings
from complexity_lab.complexity.transitions import recent_acceleration_summary, tipping_summary

con = duckdb.connect(str(settings.db_path), read_only=True)
mp = con.execute(
    "SELECT state_code, year, month, ev_share FROM panel_state_month "
    "WHERE state_code <> 'ALL' ORDER BY state_code, year, month").df()

tips_full = tipping_summary(mp, "ev_share")
policy_era = mp[(mp["year"] < 2024) | ((mp["year"] == 2024) & (mp["month"] <= 3))]
tips_policy = tipping_summary(policy_era, "ev_share")
tips_recent = tipping_summary(mp[mp["year"] >= 2022], "ev_share")
annual = con.execute(
    "SELECT state_code, year, ev_share FROM panel_state_year "
    "WHERE state_code <> 'ALL' AND year <= 2025 ORDER BY state_code, year"
).df()
momentum = recent_acceleration_summary(annual, "ev_share")
```

## The verdict across states

```{python}
def classify(t):
    if t["sse_gain"] < 0.1:
        return "no clear threshold"
    return "tipping (accelerates)" if t["hinge_coef"] > 0 else "saturation (decelerates)"

summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "full series": tips_full.apply(classify, axis=1).value_counts(),
    "policy era (≤ Mar 2024)": tips_policy.apply(classify, axis=1).value_counts(),
    "recent era (2022+)": tips_recent.apply(classify, axis=1).value_counts(),
}).fillna(0).astype(int)
summary
```

This threshold test is not a test of whether the latest annual growth rate rose.
In 2025, the observed EV-share gain accelerated versus 2024 in:

```{python}
momentum["momentum_verdict"].value_counts().to_frame("states")
```

```{python}
sat = tips_full[(tips_full["hinge_coef"] < 0) & (tips_full["sse_gain"] > 0.1)].copy()
sat["tau_pct"] = sat["tau"] * 100
fig = px.scatter(sat.reset_index(), x="tau_pct", y="sse_gain", text="state_code",
                 size="max_share", title="Saturation thresholds: where EV growth bends down",
                 labels={"tau_pct": "τ* — EV share where growth decelerates (%)",
                         "sse_gain": "threshold model improvement (SSE gain)"})
fig.update_traces(textposition="top center")
fig.show()
```

## Threshold level vs income & infrastructure

```{python}
cov = con.execute(
    "SELECT state_code, real_pc_income_inr, ev_chargers_2025, "
    "broad_credit_per_capita_inr, latest_real_gsdp_growth_pct "
    "FROM experiment_state_context").df().set_index("state_code")
j = sat.join(cov)
fig = px.scatter(j.reset_index(), x="real_pc_income_inr", y="tau_pct",
                 size="ev_chargers_2025", text="state_code", log_x=True,
                 title="Richer states bend later: saturation threshold vs per-capita income",
                 labels={"real_pc_income_inr": "real per-capita NSDP (2011-12 INR, log)",
                         "tau_pct": "τ* (%)"})
fig.update_traces(textposition="top center")
fig.show()
print("Spearman corr(τ*, income):",
      round(float(j["tau_pct"].corr(j["real_pc_income_inr"], method="spearman")), 3))
```

## Finding

Across 30 scanned states, the full-history and FAME-II hinge models find **no
robust self-reinforcing share threshold** at the stated 10% model-gain rule.
That does **not** mean no state is accelerating: most states recorded a faster
annual EV-share gain in 2025 than in 2024. These are different claims.

The structural result is window-sensitive. The 2022+ and 2023+ scans can surface
positive hinges in selected states, but the set is not stable enough to call a
national tipping law. Negative hinges in the full history are evidence of
deceleration conditional on share, not proof that the current market has stopped
accelerating in calendar time.

**Policy reading**: current acceleration is real, but a universal autonomous
tipping point is not established. Raising the ceiling through price parity and
charging density remains more defensible than assuming adoption will now run
without continued support.
